title: Lexicographically Smallest String After Adjacent Removals
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: C proposal for this challenge: the minimum-footprint champion. Recommended and speed proposals are in C++.

variants.languageReason: The memory objective favors C because the LeetCode input is a char* and all state can be represented as fixed primitive arrays plus one allocated result buffer, avoiding standard-library container and managed-runtime overhead.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Best for the recommended and runtime variants, but strings, array wrappers, and optional comparison tables raise the memory constant compared with direct C arrays.
Selected. It gives the smallest runtime/container baseline and direct control over the output allocation.
Can be memory-efficient, but ownership and string return plumbing add implementation overhead without improving asymptotic memory.
Slices are compact enough for acceptance, but the runtime and GC baseline exceed C for a memory champion.
Primitive arrays avoid boxing, but managed-runtime baseline and object metadata are higher than C.
Object-heavy strings and arrays are dominated for minimum memory.

variants.approachJustification: Use fixed 64-bit interval masks for removability and store suffix answers as linked choices. Equal-leading candidates are compared by walking those links, avoiding intermediate strings and the O(n^2) comparison table.

reasoningSummary: The C memory variant uses the same interval-DP proof as the C++ versions, but stores only fixed bit masks and linked suffix choices. It trades slower linked comparisons for lower allocation and container overhead.
